does: This paper introduces a formal specification language for ML kernel contracts that precisely captures what a kernel computes across heterogeneous hardware. A contract consists of eight components: identifier, scope, precondition, postcondition, tolerance, reference oracle, measurement protocol, and violation signature. The authors systematize twelve contract classes covering precision errors, ordering violations, compiler-induced bugs, and exceptional-value failures, each grounded in empirical evidence from published work. The key contribution is providing a formal framework to arbitrate disagreements when kernels produce different results across AMD, NVIDIA, and other platforms, moving from implicit and undocumented assumptions to explicit, checkable specifications.
connects: This work responds to recent empirical studies quantifying kernel correctness gaps across silicon platforms and compiler stacks, which documented discrepancies but lacked formal grounding. It extends prior work in numerical correctness specifications and floating-point semantics literature by applying these ideas specifically to the ML kernel domain. The paper builds on software verification traditions of contracts and formal specifications, adapting them to heterogeneous ML systems where reference implementations and ground truth are ambiguous. It connects to broader efforts in ML systems reliability and the growing awareness that infrastructure bugs silently corrupt model training and inference, problems previously addressed only through ad-hoc testing or case-by-case documentation.
enables: This contract language enables automated verification tools and testing frameworks that can validate whether kernel implementations satisfy stated specifications across different hardware targets. It provides a foundation for building kernel registries or certification systems where hardware vendors and library developers can formally document and verify their implementations' correctness properties. The work opens pathways for developing symbolic or dynamic verification techniques that check contract satisfaction systematically. It also supports better debugging workflows where kernel disagreements can be mapped to specific contract violations rather than dismissed as platform idiosyncracies, potentially driving hardware and compiler improvements toward standardized correctness guarantees in ML infrastructure.
